pattern: A path from the bottom-left to the top-right corner is blocked exactly when some chain of touching circles forms an unbroken wall from the top-or-left boundary to the bottom-or-right boundary, so the whole geometry question collapses into a union-find connectivity check between two virtual 'wall' groups.
hints: Any path has to squeeze past either the top-left pair of edges or the bottom-right pair of edges; a circle only matters if it touches one of the four rectangle edges or overlaps another circle that does.

Group circles into two virtual buckets -- one for 'touches the top edge or the left edge', one for 'touches the bottom edge or the right edge' -- and union two circles together whenever their distance is at most the sum of their radii.

The corner path exists exactly when those two virtual buckets never end up in the same union-find component; watch for integer overflow, since squared distances need coordinates up to 1e9, so keep every comparison in exact (unrounded) integer arithmetic.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, single circle clipping only the bottom-right route
statement example, one circle spans both the left and bottom edges at once
statement example, two overlapping circles chain a top-left circle to a bottom-right one
statement example, circle sits entirely outside the rectangle and touches nothing
minimum-size rectangle with a circle fully interior, touching no edge
a circle touching only the left edge alone must not block the path by itself
one circle on the top-left wall and another on the bottom-right wall, but they never touch each other
a circle near the origin corner large enough to cover it, blocking both the left and bottom edges at once

stress.description: a mix of tightly clustered circles near the rectangle's center plus scattered circles across a huge coordinate range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    x_corner = 1000000000
    y_corner = 1000000000
    circles = []
    cluster_n = m // 2
    cx0, cy0 = 500000000, 500000000
    for _ in range(cluster_n):
        cx = max(1, min(999999999, cx0 + rng.randint(-1000, 1000)))
        cy = max(1, min(999999999, cy0 + rng.randint(-1000, 1000)))
        r = rng.randint(500, 1500)
        circles.append([cx, cy, r])
    for _ in range(m - cluster_n):
        cx = rng.randint(1, 999999999)
        cy = rng.randint(1, 999999999)
        r = rng.randint(1, 2000000)
        circles.append([cx, cy, r])
    return [x_corner, y_corner, circles]
stress.note: clustered circles exercise deep union-find chains while scattered ones test the O(m^2) pairwise overlap scan at the full 1000-circle constraint
